title: The Book of Lambspring
subtitle: A Noble Ancient Philosopher on the Philosophical Stone — Fifteen Emblematic Figures of the One Substance

summary.one_line: A late 16th-century alchemical emblem book in verse, presenting 15 figures of the alchemical process through vivid animal symbolism: two fishes, a black beast, a deer and unicorn, two lions, a wolf...
summary.scope: Covers Figures I-VI of the Book of Lambspring: the Two Fishes (body-soul-spirit), the Black Beast (putrefaction), the Deer and Unicorn (soul and spirit), Two Lions (male and female), the Wolf and Dog (solution and coagulation), and the Venomous Dragon (the great medicine).

sources.label: The Book of Lambspring (Barnaud translation, alchemywebsite.com)

body.text: BE WARNED AND UNDERSTAND TRULY THAT TWO FISHES ARE SWIMMING IN OUR SEA. / The Sea is the Body, the two Fishes are Soul and Spirit. The Sages will tell you / That two fishes are in our sea / Without any flesh or bones. / Let them be cooked in their own water; / Then they also will become a vast sea, / The vastness of which no man can describe. / Moreover, the Sages say / That the two fishes are only one, not two; / They are two, and nevertheless they are one, / Body, Spirit, and Soul.

HERE YOU STRAIGHTWAY BEHOLD A BLACK BEAST IN THE FOREST. / Putrefaction. The Sage says / That a wild beast is in the forest, / Whose skin is of the blackest dye. / If any man cut off his head, / His blackness will disappear, / And give place to a snowy white. / Understand well the meaning of this head: / The blackness is called the head of the Raven; / As soon as it disappears, / A white colour is straightway manifested; / It is given this name, despoiled of its head.

THIS SURELY IS A GREAT MIRACLE AND WITHOUT ANY DECEPTION — THAT IN A VENOMOUS DRAGON THERE SHOULD BE THE GREAT MEDICINE. / A savage Dragon lives in the forest, / Most venomous he is, yet lacking nothing: / When he sees the rays of the Sun and its bright fire, / He scatters abroad his poison, / And flies upward so fiercely / That no living creature can stand before him ... / He who hath skill to slay him, wisely / Hath escaped from all dangers. / Yet all venom, and colours, are multiplied / In the hour of his death. / His venom becomes the great Medicine. / He quickly consumes his venom, / For he devours his poisonous tail. / All this is performed on his own body, / From which flows forth glorious Balm.

The two fishes swimming in the sea — 'without any flesh or bones' — are iḍā and piṅgalā, the two nāḍīs that flow in the body of the sea (the suṣumṇā). They are 'two and nevertheless one' because iḍā and piṅgalā are the two aspects of a single energy (the lunar and solar principles). The sea (the body) 'cooked in its own water' is the practice of prāṇāyāma, where the movement of prāṇa creates an internal heat (tapas) that transforms the subtle body. 'Cooking' is the Layayoga process of mīlana (fusion), where the two currents merge into suṣumṇā and become the 'vast sea' of expanded consciousness (samudra, the ocean of awareness in Tantraloka III.89). In Steiner's terms, the two fishes are the etheric body (life-force) and astral body (soul) that must be 'cooked' — purified by the 'I' — before they can unite into the Spirit Self (the one).

The Black Beast 'whose skin is of the blackest dye' whose head must be cut off for the whiteness to appear is the Guardian of the Threshold in Steiner's description — the shadow that the aspirant must confront and decapitate. The 'forest' where the beast lives is the astral plane (the 'forest' of desires and passions). Cutting off the head is not killing the beast but removing its power — the head is the 'head of the Raven,' the lead caput mortuum of the alchemists, the dross of consciousness that must be separated. In the Tibetan Book of the Dead, the peaceful and wrathful deities in the second bardo appear terrifying — the 'black beast' — but if recognized as projections of one's own mind, they 'disappear and give place to a snowy white' (the Clear Light). The 'whiteness' is not a negation of the beast but its transmutation: what was black (tamas, ignorance) becomes white (sattva, wisdom). In Tantraloka, the pañca-kléśa (five afflictions) when purified become the pañca-mudrā (five seals of wisdom).

The venomous dragon who 'when he sees the rays of the Sun and its bright fire, scatters abroad his poison, and flies upward so fiercely' is the kuṇḍalinī-śakti. She lies coiled (the 'savage Dragon') in the 'forest' of mūlādhāra. When she 'sees the rays of the Sun' (the awakening initiated by the guru's grace or by sustained practice), she 'scatters poison' — releasing the stored karmic impressions (saṃskāras) — and 'flies upward' through the suṣumṇā. The 'slaying' of the dragon is the piercing of the cakras: each cakra pierced releases more energy but also more 'venom' (purification crisis). The dragon 'devours his poisonous tail' — this is the ouroboros of the practice: the śakti after reaching sahasrāra returns back down, consuming the remaining poisons (avidyā, the primordial ignorance). The 'glorious Balm' that flows from the dragon's body is the amṛta (nectar of immortality) that floods the practitioner after the kuṇḍalinī has been stabilized in the crown — the same 'Balm of Sulphur' that Paracelsus and Ripley describe as the 'Radical Moisture' of the Stone.

gaps: Only Figures I, II, and VI covered. Figures III (Deer/Unicorn), IV (Two Lions), V (Wolf/Dog), and VII-XV not yet excerpted
Deeper analysis of the animal symbolism in alchemical, mythological, and astrological context needed

issues.title: Only 3 of 15 figures covered
issues.description: The Book of Lambspring contains 15 figures. Only Figures I, II, and VI are excerpted. Figures III-V and VII-XV await integration.

issues.title: Historical authorship of Lambspring uncertain
issues.description: The text claims authorship by 'Lambspring' (or 'Lambsprinck') but is often associated with Nicholas Barnaud who translated it. The original date and provenance are unclear.

versions.summary: Initial RO covering Figures I, II, VI of the Book of Lambspring: Two Fishes, Black Beast, and Venomous Dragon.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Book of Lambspring source
